text:
HEMATOLOGY/ONCOLOGY FOLLOW-UP NOTE
Attending Physician: GI Oncology Service

---

CHIEF COMPLAINT:
Mr. Raymond Kowalski is a 58-year-old male with KRAS G12D-mutant, MSS Stage IV sigmoid colon adenocarcinoma with synchronous bilobar hepatic metastases, presenting today for restaging review and treatment planning following completion of 12 cycles of mFOLFOX6 plus bevacizumab.

---

HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS:

Mr. Kowalski is a pleasant 58-year-old former smoker (20 pack-year history, quit 8 years ago) with Stage IV sigmoid colon cancer, initially diagnosed approximately 6 months ago when he presented with hematochezia, altered bowel habits, and a 12-pound unintentional weight loss. Colonoscopy at that time identified a partially obstructing 4.5 cm sigmoid mass; biopsy confirmed moderately differentiated adenocarcinoma. Staging CT and PET-CT revealed synchronous bilobar hepatic metastases in four segments (II, IV, VI, and VII; largest 3.8 cm in segment VI) with no pulmonary or peritoneal involvement at that time. Tumor molecular profiling identified KRAS G12D mutation (VAF 42.3%), TP53 R248W, APC truncating mutation, SMAD4 R361H, and PIK3CA E545K co-alteration; MSS by PCR; TMB 6.2 mut/Mb (low). RAS-mutant status precluded anti-EGFR therapy.

The patient was initiated on mFOLFOX6 (oxaliplatin 85 mg/m2, leucovorin 400 mg/m2, 5-FU 400 mg/m2 bolus + 2400 mg/m2 over 46 hours) plus bevacizumab 5 mg/kg every 14 days with a stated intent of converting hepatic metastases to resectability, or at minimum achieving durable disease control. The primary sigmoid tumor was left in situ, as the patient declined diverting colostomy and was managing adequately on a high-fiber diet given only partial luminal obstruction.

Mid-treatment restaging at cycle 6 demonstrated excellent partial response: hepatic lesions reduced 45-55% in linear dimension, primary sigmoid mass reduced approximately 31%, with CEA falling from 187.4 to 54.2 ng/mL and CA 19-9 falling from 412 to 138 U/mL. The case was brought to multidisciplinary tumor board at that time; the hepatic surgery team felt that ongoing response might render the hepatic disease fully resectable, though the segment II lesion's proximity to the left hepatic vein posed margin concerns.

Since cycle 6, the patient continued on cycles 7-12 with several important toxicity events. After cycle 9, he developed Grade 3 oxaliplatin-induced peripheral neuropathy - severe paresthesias and numbness in both hands and feet, inability to button shirts or use a keyboard comfortably, and mild gait instability on uneven surfaces. EMG/nerve conduction studies confirmed a moderate length-dependent axonal sensorimotor polyneuropathy consistent with platinum-induced injury. Oxaliplatin was dose-reduced from 85 mg/m2 to 65 mg/m2 beginning with cycle 10. Duloxetine was initiated at 30 mg and uptitrated to 60 mg daily for neuropathic pain management. 5-FU and bevacizumab were maintained at full doses. Additionally, bevacizumab-associated hypertension has been an ongoing management challenge: blood pressure was 152/96 mmHg after cycle 3 (requiring uptitration of lisinopril from 10 to 20 mg daily), and subsequently worsened to 162/104 mmHg after cycle 9 (requiring addition of amlodipine 10 mg daily). The patient is currently maintained on lisinopril 20 mg daily and amlodipine 10 mg daily for blood pressure management.

Today he presents to review end-of-induction imaging (CT chest/abdomen/pelvis with contrast, obtained after cycle 12) and to determine the next phase of treatment. He reports his overall functional status remains good. He continues teaching full-time (high school history teacher), though he does note the neuropathy has made writing on the chalkboard and handling papers more challenging. He reports mild residual fatigue, intermittent nausea that is well-controlled with as-needed ondansetron, and some ongoing cold sensitivity, though this has improved since the oxaliplatin dose reduction. He denies new dyspnea, chest pain, abdominal pain, or change in bowel habits. He is aware of his incurable diagnosis and remains highly motivated for aggressive treatment.

REVIEW OF SYSTEMS:

Constitutional: Mild fatigue (Grade 1-2). No fevers, chills, or night sweats. Appetite improved compared to diagnosis; no significant weight loss recently.
Neurologic: Persistent bilateral distal hand and foot paresthesias and numbness, primarily fingertips and toes. Improved from peak severity (Grade 3 at cycle 9) to current Grade 2 level following dose reduction. Duloxetine providing moderate relief. Mild residual cold sensitivity in hands. Gait stability improved on even surfaces; some caution on stairs.
Cardiovascular: Blood pressure has been elevated; two antihypertensive agents in use. No chest pain, palpitations, or lower extremity edema.
Gastrointestinal: Bowel function near baseline. No hematochezia or melena recently. No severe mucositis. Mild mouth soreness occasionally on days 2-3 post-5-FU infusion.
Dermatologic: Mild hand-foot skin reaction, Grade 1, manageable with moisturizing cream. Grade 1 alopecia (thinning).
Psychosocial: Reports stress related to illness course and work demands, but overall mood is maintained. Wife is primary support. Has found the duloxetine helpful both for neuropathy and mood.
All other systems reviewed and negative.

PHYSICAL EXAMINATION:

Vital Signs: Blood pressure 148/92 mmHg (right arm, seated), heart rate 74 bpm (regular), respiratory rate 16/min, temperature 98.2°F, oxygen saturation 97% on room air, weight 79.1 kg (stable from last visit), BMI 28.1 kg/m2.

General: Well-appearing middle-aged male, in no acute distress. Alert and oriented x3. Mildly fatigued appearing but conversant and engaged.

HEENT: Normocephalic, atraumatic. Mild oral mucosal dryness noted; no frank mucositis at present. No cervical or supraclavicular lymphadenopathy.

Cardiovascular: Regular rate and rhythm. No murmurs, rubs, or gallops. No lower extremity edema.

Pulmonary: Clear to auscultation bilaterally. No wheeze or crackles.

Abdomen: Soft, non-distended. Mild tenderness to deep palpation in the left lower quadrant (chronic, attributed to sigmoid tumor). No hepatomegaly appreciated. No ascites. Bowel sounds present and normoactive. No palpable abdominal masses.

Extremities: No clubbing or cyanosis. Trace bilateral ankle edema (new from prior visit, likely related to amlodipine). Peripheral pulses intact.

Neurologic: Cranial nerves II-XII grossly intact. Motor strength 5/5 bilaterally upper and lower extremities. Sensation: Reduced light touch and vibration sense in bilateral toes and distal feet to the ankle level; reduced light touch in bilateral fingertips. Romberg mildly positive. Tandem gait mildly unsteady. Deep tendon reflexes: 1+ at bilateral Achilles; 2+ at bilateral patellar and biceps tendons. No ataxia on standard gait testing.

Skin: Grade 1 hand-foot skin changes, mild erythema and mild scaling of palmar and plantar surfaces. Mild diffuse hair thinning. Port site (left chest) clean, no erythema or discharge.

---

LABORATORY RESULTS:

Complete Blood Count:
- WBC: 5.2 x 10^9/L (normal)
- Hemoglobin: 10.8 g/dL (mild anemia, stable, attributed to chemotherapy and chronic disease)
- Platelets: 218 x 10^9/L (normal)
- ANC: 2.4 x 10^9/L (adequate)

Comprehensive Metabolic Panel:
- Sodium: 138 mEq/L, Potassium: 4.1 mEq/L, Chloride: 103 mEq/L, Bicarbonate: 24 mEq/L
- BUN: 18 mg/dL, Creatinine: 1.1 mg/dL, eGFR: 71 mL/min/1.73m2 (stable chronic mild CKD, likely hypertensive nephropathy)
- AST: 34 U/L, ALT: 41 U/L, Total Bilirubin: 0.8 mg/dL, Alkaline Phosphatase: 112 U/L (mildly elevated, attributed to hepatic metastases)
- Albumin: 3.6 g/dL
- Glucose: 112 mg/dL (fasting, consistent with metformin-controlled T2DM)
- Magnesium: 1.6 mg/dL (low-normal; to be repleted PO)

Tumor Markers:
- CEA: 28.6 ng/mL (down from 54.2 ng/mL at cycle 6 restaging, and from 187.4 ng/mL at diagnosis)
- CA 19-9: 72 U/mL (down from 138 U/mL at cycle 6 restaging, and from 412 U/mL at diagnosis)

HbA1c: 6.9% (adequate glycemic control on metformin)
TSH: 2.1 mIU/L (normal)

---

IMAGING RESULTS:

End-of-induction CT chest/abdomen/pelvis with contrast (after cycle 12 mFOLFOX6 plus bevacizumab):

Hepatic lesions show continued, albeit slowed, response since the cycle 6 restaging. The segment VI lesion, which was 3.8 cm at diagnosis and 2.1 cm at cycle 6, has now shrunk further to 1.6 x 1.3 cm. The segment II lesion (originally 1.9 cm) measures 0.6 x 0.5 cm and is essentially stable since the cycle 6 restaging. The segment IV lesion (originally 2.2 cm) now measures 0.8 x 0.6 cm, also stable. The segment VII lesion, originally 1.1 cm, is no longer measurable and appears to represent a near-complete or complete response of that individual lesion. The primary sigmoid mass has further decreased from 3.2 cm (at cycle 6) to approximately 2.4 x 1.9 cm. No new hepatic lesions are identified. No pulmonary nodules are seen on the chest component. No peritoneal implants, no adrenal lesions, and no osseous disease identified. The cumulative tumor shrinkage from baseline represents roughly a 58% reduction across measurable hepatic target lesions. CEA has fallen from 187.4 at baseline to 28.6 ng/mL; CA 19-9 from 412 to 72 U/mL.

The case was re-reviewed at multidisciplinary tumor board this week. The hepatic surgery team acknowledges that the hepatic disease remains technically resectable, with the caveat that the residual segment II lesion's proximity to the left hepatic vein continues to present margin concerns. Adequate future liver remnant is estimated. However, the primary sigmoid tumor has not been resected; simultaneous or staged colorectal and hepatic resection was discussed. Given the patient's ongoing neuropathy, the cumulative treatment course, and the patient's own stated preference to continue systemic therapy in the context of continued tumor response, the surgical team agrees that deferring resection at this time is reasonable.

---

ASSESSMENT AND PLAN:

1. KRAS G12D-mutant, MSS, Stage IV sigmoid colon adenocarcinoma with bilobar hepatic metastases - partial response maintained after 12 cycles of mFOLFOX6 plus bevacizumab.

The patient has completed 12 cycles of induction mFOLFOX6 plus bevacizumab and has achieved a meaningful, durable partial response - his hepatic disease has shrunk by more than half and his tumor markers have fallen to roughly 15% of their original values. This is a gratifying result. However, continuation of oxaliplatin beyond 12 cycles in the setting of established Grade 2-3 cumulative neuropathy (which only partially reversed with dose reduction) is not appropriate. We had anticipated this transition and the plan to de-escalate from oxaliplatin while maintaining disease control is consistent with the OPTIMOX1 strategy.

After discussion with the patient and review of the imaging and toxicity data, the multidisciplinary team and patient are aligned on the following:

- TRANSITION TO MAINTENANCE THERAPY: We will discontinue oxaliplatin and transition to maintenance simplified LV5FU2 (leucovorin 400 mg/m2 IV day 1, 5-FU 400 mg/m2 IV bolus followed by 2400 mg/m2 continuous infusion over 46 hours) plus bevacizumab 5 mg/kg IV every 14 days. This approach preserves the 5-FU backbone and anti-angiogenic activity while eliminating further cumulative oxaliplatin neurotoxicity. Expect that peripheral neuropathy will gradually improve off oxaliplatin over the coming weeks to months.

- HEPATIC RESECTION: Deferred at this time per patient preference and ongoing response. Will reassess at subsequent restaging. If tumor markers plateau or begin to rise on maintenance therapy without imaging evidence of response, we will re-engage surgical oncology for hepatic resection evaluation. Colorectal and hepatic resection sequence to be discussed with the surgical team if and when this becomes relevant.

- RESTAGING: Plan to restage with CT abdomen/pelvis with contrast (CT chest as clinically indicated) every 8-10 weeks on maintenance therapy. Will obtain repeat CEA and CA 19-9 at each visit.

- FUTURE SECOND-LINE CONSIDERATIONS: Patient is aware that KRAS G12D represents a targetable alteration with emerging investigational therapies. No approved KRAS G12D inhibitor for CRC exists at this time. Patient's enrollment information in the KRAS G12D trial registry has been maintained. If and when disease progresses on maintenance, second-line therapy would favor an irinotecan-based regimen, likely FOLFIRI, with a vascular-targeted agent (ramucirumab or bevacizumab continuation per applicable trial data). TAS-102 and regorafenib held for later lines.

2. Grade 2 oxaliplatin-induced peripheral neuropathy, improving.
   - Duloxetine 60 mg daily continued.
   - Expect gradual improvement following oxaliplatin discontinuation; patient counseled that full recovery may take many months and may be incomplete.
   - Will monitor neuropathy at each visit; repeat EMG/NCS in 3-4 months to document trajectory.

3. Bevacizumab-associated hypertension, Grade 2-3.
   - Current BP 148/92 mmHg on lisinopril 20 mg daily and amlodipine 10 mg daily - improved from recent peaks but still above goal.
   - Continue dual antihypertensive therapy. Home blood pressure monitoring twice daily (mornings and evenings) and log to bring to next visit.
   - If systolic BP remains consistently above 150 mmHg at next visit, will consider adding a low-dose thiazide or uptitrating current agents in coordination with primary care.
   - Renal function monitored (eGFR stable at 71).

4. Type 2 diabetes mellitus.
   - Well-controlled on metformin 1000 mg BID; HbA1c 6.9%.
   - Continue current regimen. Renal function stable; metformin appropriate at current eGFR.

5. Hypertension (pre-existing, now complicated by bevacizumab therapy).
   - See above.

6. Mild normocytic anemia.
   - Hemoglobin 10.8 g/dL, stable. Attributed to chemotherapy and chronic inflammation. No iron deficiency noted. No transfusion needed at this time. Will continue to monitor; may improve off oxaliplatin.

7. Hypomagnesemia (mild).
   - Prescribing magnesium oxide 400 mg PO twice daily. Recheck magnesium in 2 weeks.

8. Goals of care.
   - Patient has a clear understanding of his diagnosis (incurable Stage IV disease) and desires continued aggressive treatment. This is consistent with his overall functional status (ECOG PS 1) and his goals. His advance directive has been updated. Wife remains his healthcare proxy. No hospice or palliative care referral at this time; patient is actively engaged in oncologic management.

Return to clinic in 2 weeks for first maintenance LV5FU2 plus bevacizumab infusion and to review blood pressure log. Labs at that visit (CBC, CMP, CEA, CA 19-9). Patient verbalized understanding of the plan and has no further questions today. He expressed relief about stopping the oxaliplatin and cautious optimism about the maintenance phase.

Time spent with patient: 55 minutes (>50% counseling and coordination of care).

Electronically signed,
GI Medical Oncology Attending
